title: Add a product
summary: Learn how to insert a new product into the inventory_items table, understand the importance of specifying columns, and practice safe data insertion in SQL.

sketchBlocks.title: Why use INSERT statements?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `INSERT` statement in SQL allows you to add new rows to a table. For example, to add a new product to the `inventory_items` table, you can use:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Enamel Pin', 'Accessories', 6.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-10');
```

This adds a new product called 'Enamel Pin' to the inventory. Notice that we specify the columns explicitly, which helps prevent mistakes if the table structure changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Explicit column lists for safety
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
It's best practice to list the columns you are inserting into. This makes your SQL more robust and readable. For example:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Desk Organizer', 'Office', 18.00, 'draft', 0, 'New office item', '2026-02-12');
```

If you omit the column list, you must provide values for every column in the table, in the correct order. This can lead to errors if the table changes.

quizDraft.title: Insert a new product row
quizDraft.prompt: Write an SQL statement to add a new product called 'Travel Journal' in the 'Accessories' category, priced at 11.99, with status 'active', is_test 0, no notes, and last_updated '2026-02-11' to the inventory_items table. Use an explicit column list (do not include the id column).
quizDraft.hint: List all columns except id, and match the order of values to the columns.

help.concept: When inserting data, specifying the column names ensures the correct mapping of values to columns.
help.hint_1: Start with INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, ...).
help.hint_2: Remember to use NULL for notes and provide the correct value for each column.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Travel Journal', 'Accessories', 11.99, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-11');

quizDraft.title: Insert multiple products at once
quizDraft.prompt:
Write an SQL statement to insert two new products into inventory_items at once:
1. 'Desk Lamp', category 'Office', price 22.50, status 'draft', is_test 0, notes 'LED', last_updated '2026-02-13'
2. 'Sample Keychain', category 'Accessories', price 3.00, status 'inactive', is_test 1, notes 'Promo item', last_updated '2026-02-14'
Do not include the id column.
quizDraft.hint: You can insert multiple rows by separating each set of values with a comma.

help.concept: SQL allows inserting multiple rows in a single INSERT statement by providing multiple sets of values.
help.hint_2: Make sure the order of values matches the column list for both rows.

quizDraft.title: Insert with default and NULL values
quizDraft.prompt: Write an SQL statement to insert a product called 'Eco Notebook' in the 'Office' category, priced at 7.25. Omit the status and is_test columns so they use their default values. Set notes to NULL and last_updated to '2026-02-15'. Do not include the id column.
quizDraft.hint: Only list the columns you want to provide values for; the rest will use defaults.

help.concept: If you omit columns with defaults in your column list, SQL will use the default values for those columns.
help.hint_1: List only name, category, price, notes, and last_updated in your column list.
help.hint_2: Do not mention status or is_test in your column list; SQL will fill them in automatically.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Eco Notebook', 'Office', 7.25, NULL, '2026-02-15');

quizDraft.title: Why specify columns in INSERT?
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are reasons to always specify column names in your INSERT statements? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what could go wrong if you don't specify columns.

help.concept: Specifying columns in INSERT statements helps prevent errors and makes your code more robust to schema changes.
help.hint_1: Consider what happens if the table structure changes or the order of columns is different.
help.hint_2: Not specifying columns can cause data to be inserted into the wrong fields or lead to errors.

options.a: It prevents errors if the table structure changes.
options.b: It makes the SQL statement easier to read and maintain.
options.c: It allows you to omit columns that have default values.
options.d: It automatically generates unique ids for new rows.
options.e: It ensures values are inserted into the correct columns.

quizDraft.title: Default values in INSERT
quizDraft.prompt: When you omit a column with a default value in your INSERT statement, SQL will use the ______ value for that column.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what SQL does when you don't provide a value for a column that has a default.

help.concept: If a column has a default value and you don't specify it in your INSERT, SQL uses the default.
help.hint_1: The value comes from the table definition, not from your statement.
help.hint_2: It's not NULL unless the default is NULL.

quizDraft.template: When you omit a column with a default value in your INSERT statement, SQL will use the {blank} value for that column.
